flashcards.front: In which Java version were private interface methods introduced?
flashcards.back: Private interface methods were introduced in Java 9 as part of the Java Platform Module System (JPMS) enhancement.

flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of private interface methods in Java?
flashcards.back: To allow code reuse within interfaces by extracting common logic from default methods without exposing that logic to implementing classes.

flashcards.front: Can a private interface method be abstract?
flashcards.back: No, private interface methods cannot be abstract. They must have a body (implementation) since they are always private and non-abstract.

flashcards.front: Can private interface methods be overridden by implementing classes?
flashcards.back: No, private interface methods are not inherited by implementing classes and cannot be overridden. They are only accessible within the interface itself.

flashcards.front: What are the access modifiers allowed for interface methods in Java 9+?
flashcards.back: Java 9+ allows public, private, static, and default modifiers for interface methods. Private methods can be non-static only.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between private and static interface methods?
flashcards.back: Private interface methods are instance methods that can be called by other default or private methods. Static interface methods belong to the interface itself and are called using InterfaceName.methodName().

flashcards.front: Can an interface have both private and default methods?
flashcards.back: Yes, an interface can have both private and default methods. Private methods are helper methods that default methods can call internally.

flashcards.front: Why were private interface methods needed despite default methods already existing in Java 8?
flashcards.back: Default methods in Java 8 led to code duplication when multiple default methods shared common logic. Private methods allow extracting this shared logic cleanly without exposing it to implementing classes.

flashcards.front: Can private interface methods access instance variables?
flashcards.back: Interfaces cannot have instance variables in Java. However, private interface methods can be called by default methods that operate on the interface's abstract behavior.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax to call a private method from a default method in an interface?
flashcards.back: Simply call the private method by its name, like calling any other instance method: privateMethodName(); No special syntax needed since both belong to the same interface.

flashcards.front: Can a private interface method throw checked exceptions?
flashcards.back: Yes, private interface methods can throw checked exceptions just like regular methods, following the same rules as other interface methods.

flashcards.front: Are private interface methods part of the public API of an interface?
flashcards.back: No, private interface methods are not part of the public API. They are implementation details hidden from outside the interface and implementing classes.

flashcards.front: Can we have multiple private methods in a single interface?
flashcards.back: Yes, an interface can have multiple private methods. These can be overloaded with different parameters and can call each other.

flashcards.front: What happens if a default method calls a non-existent private method?
flashcards.back: It results in a compile-time error. The compiler checks that all method calls within the interface are valid.

flashcards.front: How do private interface methods help in achieving encapsulation?
flashcards.back: They allow hiding internal implementation details and helper logic within the interface, keeping the public API clean while maintaining code organization.
